channel.title: Univalent Foundations on Jon Sterling's Forest
channel.description: Recent content in Univalent Foundations on Jon Sterling's Forest

item.title: quasi-idempotent in a wild category
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category; a quasi-idempotent on an object {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#} is defined to be a pre-idempotent {#\prn{f,\phi}#} on {#A#} together with an identification {#\chi#} between {#f;\phi : f;\prn{f;f} = f;f#} and the following composite identification: {## f;\prn{f;f} \xrightarrow{\Con{assoc}\Sub{\CCat},f,f,f} \prn{f;f};f \xrightarrow{\phi;f} f;f ##}

item.title: pre-idempotent in a wild category
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category; a pre-idempotent on an object {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#} is defined to be a morphism {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{A}#} together with an identification {#\phi : f;f = f#}.

item.title: initial objects in wild categories
item.description: Lemma (wild initial objects from cones with trivial generic element) [jms-003B] Let \CCat be a wild category, and let I:\Ob{\CCat} be an object of \CCat . If we may equip a wild natural transformation \Mor{\alpha}{\Delta I}{\Idn{\CCat}} from the constant 0-functor \Mor{\Delta I}{\CCat}{\CCat} to the identity 0-functor \Mor{\Idn{\CCat}}{\CCat}{\CCat} with an identification c_\alpha : \alpha_I = \Idn{I} , then \Hom{\CCat}{I}{A} is contractible for all A:\Ob{\CCat} . (Our proof will follow that of the nLab, which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones.

item.title: wild natural transformations
item.description: Definition (wild natural transformation) [jms-0039] Let \CCat,\DCat be two wild categories, and let F,G:\CCat\to\DCat be two wild 0-functors from \CCat to \DCat . A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data: for each A:\Ob{\CCat} , an element \alpha_{A} : \Hom{\DCat}{\Ob{F}A}{\Ob{G}A} ; for each f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B} , an identification \alpha_{f} : \alpha_A; \Homs{G}{f} = \Homs{F}{f};\alpha_B . Definition (wild semi-coherent natural transformations) [jms-003H] Let \CCat,\DCat be two wild categories, and let F,G:\CCat\to\DCat be two wild 1-semi-functors from \CCat to \DCat .

item.title: wild functors
item.description: Definition (wild 0-functor) [jms-0038] Let \CCat and \DCat be two wild categories. A wild 0-functor from \CCat to \DCat is given by the following data: a function \Ob{F} : \Ob{\CCat}\to\Ob{\DCat} ; for each A,B:\Ob{\CCat} , a function \Homs{F} : \Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}\to\Hom{\DCat}{\Ob{F}A}{\Ob{F}B} . In other words, a wild 0-functor is a homomorphism of the underlying graphs. Definition (wild 1-semi-functor) [jms-003F] Let \CCat and \DCat be two wild categories. A wild 1-semi-functor from \CCat to \DCat is given by a wild 0-functor F : \CCat\to\DCat equipped with, for each f:\Hom{\CCat}{C}{D} and g : \Hom{\CCat}{C}{D} , an identification \Con{seq}_F\,f\,g : \Homs{F}\prn{f;g} = \Homs{F}{f};\Homs{G}{g} .

item.title: wild semi-coherent natural transformations
item.description: Let {#\CCat,\DCat#} be two wild categories, and let {#F,G:\CCat\to\DCat#} be two wild 1-semi-functors from {#\CCat#} to {#\DCat#}. A wild semi-coherent natural transformation is defined by the data of a wild natural transformation {#\alpha : F \to G#} together with, for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#} and {#g : \Hom{\CCat}{B}{C}#}, a higher identification {#\Con{seq}\Sub{\alpha},f,g#} between {#\alpha\Sub{f;g} : F\prn{f;g};\alpha\Sub{C} = \alpha\Sub{A};G\prn{f;g}#} and the composite identification:

item.title: wild 1-semi-functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} and {#\DCat#} be two wild categories. A wild 1-semi-functor from {#\CCat#} to {#\DCat#} is given by a wild 0-functor {#F : \CCat\to\DCat#} equipped with, for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{C}{D}#} and {#g : \Hom{\CCat}{C}{D}#}, an identification {#\Con{seq}_F,f,g : \Homs{F}\prn{f;g} = \Homs{F}{f};\Homs{G}{g} #}.

item.title: splitting the generic element of a cone
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category, and let {#I:\Ob{\CCat}#} be an object of {#\CCat#} such that {#\Mor{\alpha}{\Delta I}{\Idn{\CCat}}#} is a wild natural transformation from the constant 0-functor {#\Mor{\Delta I}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} to the identity 0-functor {#\Mor{\Idn{\CCat}}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} on {#\CCat#} for some {#I:\Ob{\CCat}#}. Now let {#r:\Hom{\CCat}{I}{J}#} and {#s:\Hom{\CCat}{J}{I}#} be a section-retraction pair witnessed by {#\eta : s;r = \Idn{I}#}, and let {#\sigma : r;s=\alpha_{I}#} be a splitting of {#\alpha_{I}:\Hom{\CCat}{I}{I}#}. Then we may define a wild natural transformation {#\Mor{\beta}{\Delta J}{\Idn{\CCat}}#} and an identification {#\chi : \beta_{J} = \Idn{J}#}.

item.title: the wild constant functor
the wild identity functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category; the identity 0-functor {#\Mor{\Idn{\CCat}}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} on {#\CCat#} is defined as follows: {## \begin{aligned} \Ob{\Idn{\CCat}}A &:\equiv A\ \Homs{\Idn{\CCat}}f &:\equiv f \end{aligned} ##} The above may be extended to a wild 1-semi-functor as follows: {## \Con{seq}_{F},f,g :\equiv \Refl ##}

item.title: wild initial objects from cones with trivial generic element
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a wild category, and let {#I:\Ob{\CCat}#} be an object of {#\CCat#}. If we may equip a wild natural transformation {#\Mor{\alpha}{\Delta I}{\Idn{\CCat}}#} from the constant 0-functor {#\Mor{\Delta I}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} to the identity 0-functor {#\Mor{\Idn{\CCat}}{\CCat}{\CCat}#} with an identification {#c_\alpha : \alpha_I = \Idn{I}#}, then {#\Hom{\CCat}{I}{A}#} is contractible for all {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#}. (Our proof will follow that of the nLab, which was stated for ordinary categories and functors and natural transformations rather than wild ones.

item.title: wild category theory
item.description: We describe some basic definitions and results in “wild (incoherent) category theory”, i.e. the study of untruncated category-like objects in univalent foundations that may lack higher coherences and saturation/completeness assumptions. Some of this is based on the Homotopy Type Theory Coq library, as well as ideas from a blog post of Mike Shulman and private conversations with Mike Shulman. Definition (wild category) [jms-0037] A wild category is given by the following data:

item.title: wild natural transformation
item.description: Let {#\CCat,\DCat#} be two wild categories, and let {#F,G:\CCat\to\DCat#} be two wild 0-functors from {#\CCat#} to {#\DCat#}. A wild natural transformation is defined by the following data: for each {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#}, an element {#\alpha_{A} : \Hom{\DCat}{\Ob{F}A}{\Ob{G}A}#}; for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#}, an identification {#\alpha_{f} : \alpha_A; \Homs{G}{f} = \Homs{F}{f};\alpha_B#}.

item.title: wild 0-functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} and {#\DCat#} be two wild categories. A wild 0-functor from {#\CCat#} to {#\DCat#} is given by the following data: a function {#\Ob{F} : \Ob{\CCat}\to\Ob{\DCat}#}; for each {#A,B:\Ob{\CCat}#}, a function {#\Homs{F} : \Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}\to\Hom{\DCat}{\Ob{F}A}{\Ob{F}B}#}. In other words, a wild 0-functor is a homomorphism of the underlying graphs.

item.title: wild category
item.description: A wild category is given by the following data: a type {#\Ob{\CCat}#}; for each {#A,B:\Ob{\CCat}#}, a family of types {#\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#}; for each {#A:\Ob{\CCat}#}, an element {#\Idn{A} : \Hom{\CCat}{A}{A}#}; for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#} and {#g:\Hom{\CCat}{B}{C}#}, an element {#\prn{f;g} : \Hom{\CCat}{A}{C}#} for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#}, a identification {#\Con{lunit}\Sub{\CCat} f : \prn{\Idn{A};f} = f#} and a identification {#\Con{runit}\Sub{\CCat} f : \prn{f;\Idn{B}}=f#}; for each {#f:\Hom{\CCat}{A}{B}#} and {#g:\Hom{\CCat}{B}{C}#} and {#h:\Hom{\CCat}{C}{D}#}, a identification {#\Con{assoc}\Sub{\CCat},f,g,h : f;\prn{g;h} = \prn{f;g};h#}.

item.title: positivity and the strictly-below relation
item.description: It follows from our characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements that an element {#a: A#} of a pointed dcpo {#A#} is positive if and only if it is strictly above the bottom element, i.e. {#\bot\StBelow a#}. (See also Proposition 6.2.12 of Tom de Jong’s thesis.)

item.title: results in constructive monad theory
item.description: In this section, we gather some useful definitions and results concerning creation of (co)limits. The reason for stating these things explicitly is that the usual definitions of created (co)limits are deeply irrational in a variety of incompatible ways, so it is important to fix definitions that can work from the start. It would be good to reformulate this in terms of displayed categories of algebras. Definition (creation of (co)limits) [jms-001H] Let \Mor{U}{\CCat}{\DCat} be a functor and let \ICat be a category.

item.title: characterization of positivity in terms of partial elements
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo. Then an element {#a:A#} is positive if and only if for any partial element {#u: LA#} such that {#a\sqsubseteq\bigsqcup_{p:u{\downarrow}}u[p]#}, we have {#u{\downarrow}=\top#}. Proof. Therefore, we first suppose that any {#u: LA#} is defined supposing {#a\sqsubseteq\bigsqcup_{p:u{\downarrow}}u[p]#} to check that {#a#} is positive. Fixing a semidirected subset {#I\subseteq A#} such that {#a\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#}, we must show that {#I#} is inhabited. We consider the partial element {#u=\prn{\exists i\in I.

item.title: the two notions of positivity coincide for pointed dcpos
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo. Then an element {#x:A#} is a positive element of the pointed dcpo if and only if {#\Mor{x}{\ObjTerm{}}{A}#} is a positive element in the dominion {#\mathcal{O}#} of Scott-open immersions of dcpos. Proof. We first observe that the latter condition is equivalent to the following: For any {#u\in LA#}, if {#a\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p: u{\downarrow}}u[p]#} then {#u{\downarrow}=\top#}. This follows from the (constructive) characterization of the path order in dcpos.

item.title: positive elements in a dominion
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion {#\mathcal{M}#} that has a Sierpiński interval {#\prn{\Sigma,\top,\bot}#}, such that {#\mathcal{M}#}-partial map classifiers {#LA#} exist for all {#A\in\CCat#}. We will write {#\mathbb{L}=\prn{L,\eta,\mu}#} for the resulting partial map classifier monad on {#\CCat#}. Let {#\prn{A,\alpha}#} be an {#\mathbb{L}#}-algebra in {#\CCat#}, and let {#\Mor{x}{C}{A}#} be a generalized element of {#A#}. We say that {#\Mor{x}{C}{A}#} is positive when for any {#\Mor{u}{C}{LA}#} such that there exists a path from {#\Mor{x}{C}{A}#} to {#\Mor{u;\alpha}{C}{A}#}, then {#\Mor{u}{C}{LA}#} factors through some (necessarily unique) {#\Mor{y}{C}{A}#} as below:

item.title: paths in a dominion
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be category with pullbacks, a terminal object, and an initial object, equipped with a dominion {#\mathcal{M}#} that has a Sierpiński interval {#\prn{\Sigma,\top,\bot}#}. A path from {#\Mor{f}{I}{C}#} to {#\Mor{g}{I}{C}#} in {#\CCat#} is defined to be a morphism {#\Mor{p}{\Sigma\times I}{C}#} such that the following diagram commutes: We shall write {#p:f\leadsto g#} to denote a path from {#f#} to {#g#}.

item.title: the Sierpiński interval of a dominion
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let {#\mathcal{M}#} be a dominion on {#\CCat#}. A Sierpiński space for {#\mathcal{M}#} is defined to be a universal {#\mathcal{M}#}-partial map, i.e. a morphism {#\Mor{\top}{\ObjTerm{\CCat}}{\Sigma}\in\mathcal{M}#} such that for any {#\Mor{i}{U}{A}\in \mathcal{M}#} there exists a unique map {#\Mor{\chi_i}{A}{\Sigma}#} making the following square cartesian: When {#\CCat#} has an initial object {#\ObjInit{\CCat}#} and every {#\Mor{}{\ObjInit{\CCat}}{A}#} lies in {#\mathcal{M}#}, the Sierpiński space becomes an “interval object” {#\prn{\Sigma,\top,\bot}#} where {#\Mor{\bot}{\ObjTerm{\CCat}}{\Sigma}#} is given as follows:

item.title: a general characterization of positivity
item.description: So far we have studied positivity as an order-theoretic property of domains; in this section, we explore a non-order-theoretic generalization of this condition that is applicable in the setting of axiomatic domain theory. This notion is inspired by the alternative characterization positivity in terms of partial elements. Definition (the Sierpiński interval of a dominion) [jms-002P] Let \CCat be a category with pullbacks, a terminal object and let \mathcal{M} be a dominion on \CCat .

item.title: positively generated elements are non-negatively generated
item.description: Let {#a#} be a positively generated element of a pointed dcpo {#A#}. Then {#a#} is also non-negatively generated. Proof. We must check that {#a\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\lnot\prn{a = \bot}}a#}. By assumption, it suffices to check that {#\bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a\sqsubseteq\bigsqcup_{p:\lnot\prn{a=\bot}}a#}, which follows because the bottom element is not positive.

item.title: positively generated basis yields positive generation
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis {#B\subseteq A#} whose elements are all positively generated in {#A#}. Then {#A#} is positively generated. Proof. As positive generation implies non-negative generation for an element of a pointed dcpo, we may conclude that {#A#} is positively generated.

item.title: when is a continuous pointed dcpo positively generated?
item.description: We describe some light conditions under which a continuous dcpo is positively generated. Lemma (non-negatively generated basis yields positive generation) [jms-0026] Let A be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis B\subseteq A such that each element of B is non-negatively generated in A . Then A is positively generated. Proof. We must show that for any a\in A , we have a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a . Let B_a be the subset \brc{b \in B \mid b\WayBelow a} .

item.title: basis with decidable bottomhood yields positive generation
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis {#B\subseteq A#} for each element {#b\in B#} it is decidable whether {#b=\bot#}. Then {#A#} is positively generated. Proof. We observe that every element of {#B#} is non-negatively generated, so we may conclude that {#A#} is positively generated.

item.title: non-negatively generated elements
item.description: An element {#a#} of a pointed dcpo {#A#} is called non-negatively generated when {#a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{\lnot\prn{a=\bot}}a#} holds.

item.title: bottom and non-bottom elements are non-negatively generated
item.description: In a pointed dcpo, any non-bottom element is non-negatively generated. The bottom element {#\bot#}. Any compact element {#a:A#} for which {#\lnot\prn{a=\bot}#} holds. Hence, any element {#a#} for which it is decidable whether {#a=\bot#} is non-negatively generated. Proof. The bottom element is clearly non-negatively generated, as it lies beneath any element. Now fix {#a:A#} and assume {#\lnot\prn{a=\bot}#}; we must check that {#a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\lnot\prn{a=\bot}}a#} but the latter is equal to {#a#}.

item.title: the bottom element is not positive
item.description: The bottom element {#\bot#} of a pointed dcpo {#A#} is not positive. Proof. Suppose that {#\bot#} was positive. The empty set {#\brc{}\subseteq A#} is semidirected and {#\bot\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup\brc{}=\bot#}, but nonetheless {#\brc{}#} is not inhabited.

item.title: the positively generated core of a dcpo
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo; the subposet {#\PGCore{A}\subseteq{A}#} spanned by positively generated elements is closed under directed suprema, and thus gives rise to a subdcpo that we shall refer to as the positively generated core of {#A#}. Proof. Let {#I\subseteq A#} be a directed subset whose elements are all positively generated. We must check that the supremum {#\bigsqcup{I}#} is positively generated, i.e. {#\bigsqcup{I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,\bigsqcup{I}}\bigsqcup{I}#}. Fixing {#i\in I#}, we must check that {#i\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,{\bigsqcup{I}}}\bigsqcup{I}#}.

item.title: positively generated elements
item.description: Let {#A#} be a dcpo; then an element {#a:A#} is called positively generated when it is the supremum of the semidirected subset {#\brc{b:A\mid b=a\land \IsPos,{a}}#}, i.e. we have {#a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}.

item.title: characterizing freeness in terms of positivity
item.description: In classical mathematics, every {#\LiftM#}-algebra is free; this does not appear to be asserted by constructive mathematics, but in this section we verify that freeness can be characterized in terms of positivity. The main result is that a pointed dcpo {#A#} is positively generated if and only the evident map {#\Mor{}{\Lift{A^+}}{A}#} is an isomorphism. Construction (the “purification” map) [jms-0021] Let A be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; we define the “purification” map \Mor{\Purify_A}{A}{\Lift{A^+}} into the lift of the subdcpo of positive elements to send a\in A to the partial element \prn{\IsPos\,{a},\lambda p.

item.title: positive generation and open positivity predicates
item.description: In a lifted dcpo {#\Lift{A}#}, the positive elements form an open subdcpo {#\prn{\Lift{A}}^+\cong A#}: thus, it follows that positivity is always an open property in classical mathematics. On the other hand, it seems that constructive mathematics does not necessarily assert that the positive elements form an open subspace in general. In this section, we grapple with this subtlety by studying the relationship between openness of {#A^+#} and a desirable property that we shall refer to as positive generation, which states that every element of {#A#} is the supremum of its “positive part”.

item.title: basic properties of positivity
item.description: Definition (the positive elements of a pointed dcpo) [jms-001M] An element u of a pointed dpco A is called positive when for any semidirected subset I\subseteq A , if u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I} then I is inhabited. We will write \IsPos\,{u} to mean that u is a positive element. (This is adapted from Johnstone’s definition of positive elements of frames to the case of dcpos by Tom de Jong.) Lemma (the bottom element is not positive) [jms-002H] The bottom element \bot of a pointed dcpo A is not positive.

item.title: liftedness = positive generation
item.description: A pointed dcpo {#A#} is lifted if and only if it is positively generated. Proof. This follows from two existing lemmas: Lifted dcpos (free algebras) are positively generated. Positively generated pointed dcpos are free.

item.title: positively generated pointed dcpos are free
item.description: Let {#A#} be a positively generated pointed dcpo; then {#A#} is canonically free on its subdcpo of positive elements {#A^+#}. More specifically, the embedding-projection pair {#\prn{\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A}\dashv\Mor{\Purify_A}{A}{\Lift{A^+}}#} given by the purification map is an isomorphism of dcpos. Proof. First, we recall that the embedding-projection pair {#\prn{\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A}\dashv\Purify_A#} exists because {#A#} has an open positivity predicate by virtue of its positive generation. Therefore, the statement of this lemma is well-formed. Because {#\prn{\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A}\dashv\Purify_A#} is an embedding-projection pair, it remains only to check that {#\Purify_A;\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A#} is an inflation, i.

item.title: positivity is upward-closed
item.description: Let {#i\sqsubseteq j#} in a pointed dcpo {#A#} such that {#i#} is positive; then {#j#} is positive. Proof. Fixing semidirected {#J\subseteq A#} such that {#j\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{J}#}, we must show that {#J#} is inhabited. This follows from our assumption that {#i#} is positive becuase {#i\sqsubseteq j\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{J}#}.

item.title: the positive part of a semidirected subset is semidirected
item.description: Let {#I\subseteq A#} be a semidirected subset of a pointed dcpo {#A#}; then the intersection {#I \cap A^+#} is semidirected, where {#A^+#} is the subdcpo spanned by positive elements. Proof. Let {#i,j#} be two positive elements of {#I#}; we must show that there exists a positive element {#k\in I#} such that {#i\sqsubseteq k#} and {#j\sqsubseteq k#}. This follows immediately from the directedness of {#I#} and the fact that positivity is monotone.

item.title: positively generated dcpos have open positivity predicates
item.description: Let {#A#} be a positively generated pointed dcpo. Then {#A#} has an open positivity predicate. Proof. Let {#I\subseteq A#} be a directed subset such that {#\bigsqcup{I}#} is positive; we must find a positive element of {#I#}. Because {#A#} is positively generated we know that {#\bigsqcup{I} \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup\prn{I\cap A^+}#}. Because {#\bigsqcup{I}#} is positive and {#I\cap A^+#} is semidirected, it follows that the latter is inhabited.

item.title: non-negatively generated basis yields positive generation
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed continuous dcpo presented by a basis {#B\subseteq A#} such that each element of {#B#} is non-negatively generated in {#A#}. Then {#A#} is positively generated. Proof. We must show that for any {#a\in A#}, we have {#a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}. Let {#B_a#} be the subset {#\brc{b \in B \mid b\WayBelow a}#}. By assumption, it suffices to check that {#\bigsqcup B_a \sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}. Fixing {#b\in B_a#}, we must check that {#b\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}.

item.title: lifted dcpos are positively generated
item.description: Let {#A#} be a dcpo; then the lift {#\Lift{A}#} is positively generated. Proof. Let {#u=\prn{\phi,a}#} be an element of {#\Lift{A}#}. Note that {#u#} is equal to {#\bigsqcup_{p:\phi}u#}; as definedness coincides with positivity in lifted dcpos, we have {#\phi = \IsPos,u#}, and so we are done.

item.title: lifted dcpos have open positivity predicates
item.description: Let {#A#} be a dcpo; then the lifting {#\Lift{A}#} has an open positivity predicate. Proof. It suffices to recall that a lifted dcpo is positively generated.

item.title: positively generated dcpos
item.description: When a pointed dcpo {#A#} satisfies either of the following equivalent conditions, we will refer to it as positively generated. Every element {#a: A#} is positively generated, i.e. we have {#a = \bigsqcup_{p:\IsPos,a}a#}. For any semidirected subset {#I\subseteq A#}, we have {#\bigsqcup I = \bigsqcup \brc{a\in I \mid \IsPos,a}#}. Proof. (2) implies (1) by setting {#I = \brc{a}#}. Conversely, assume that each element is the supremum of its positive part and fix a semidirected subset {#I\subseteq A#}.

item.title: open positivity predicate
item.description: We say that a pointed dcpo {#A#} has an open positivity predicate if and only if any of the following equivalent conditions hold: The positivity predicate {#\Mor{\IsPos}{\Gamma A}{\Omega}#} tracks a continuous morphism of dcpos {#\Mor{\IsPos}{A}{\Sigma}#}. The embedding {#\Mor{\iota^+}{A^+}{A}#} is a Scott-open immersion. For a directed subset {#I\subseteq A#}, if {#\bigsqcup{A}#} is positive then there exists an element {#a\in I#} that is positive in {#A#}.

item.title: the "purification" map
pointed dcpos with open positivity predicates are almost free
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo with an open positivity predicate; then the purification map {#\Mor{\Purify_A}{A}{\Lift{A^+}}#} is the projection part of an embedding-projection pair {#\prn{\Lift{\iota^+};\alpha_A}\dashv \Purify_A#}. Proof. We fix {#\prn{\phi,a^+}: \Lift{A^+}#} and compute explicitly using the fact that {#\Purify_A#} preserves the bottom element and therefore preserves semidirected subsets: {## \begin{aligned} \Purify_A\prn{\alpha_A\prn{\Lift{\iota^+}\prn{\phi,a^+}}} &= \Purify_A\prn{\alpha_A\prn{\phi,a^+}} \ &= \Purify_A\bigsqcup_{p:\phi} a^+p \ &= \bigsqcup_{p:\phi} \Purify_A\prn{a^+p} \ &= \bigsqcup_{p:\phi} \bigsqcup_{q:\IsPos\prn{a^+p}} a^+p \ &= \bigsqcup_{p:\phi} a^+p \ &= \prn{\phi,a^+} \end{aligned} ##}

item.title: pointed dcpo
item.description: A dcpo is called pointed when it has a bottom element {#\bot#} under the information order.

item.title: the subdcpo spanned by positive elements
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo; then the subposet {#A^+\subseteq A#} spanned by positive is a dcpo. Proof. This follows from the closure of positive elements in an algebra under directed suprema.

item.title: closure of positivity under directed suprema
item.description: Let {#A#} be a pointed dcpo; the positivity predicate {#\Mor{\IsPos}{\Gamma A}{\Omega}#} is closed under directed suprema. Proof. Let {#J\subseteq A#} be a directed subset such that every {#u\in J#} is positive; we must show that {#\bigsqcup{J}#} is positive. Fixing a subdirected subset {#I\subseteq A#} such that {#\bigsqcup{J}\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I#}, we must check that {#I#} is inhabited. The inequality {#\bigsqcup{J}\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup I#} holds if and only if {#u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#} for all {#u\in u#}; as {#J#} is directed, we therefore have some positive {#u\in A#} such that {#u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#} from which it follows that {#I#} is inhabited.

item.title: positivity = definedness in a lifted dcpo
item.description: The positive elements of a lifted dcpo {#\Lift{A}#} are exactly the defined elements, i.e. those lying in the image of {#\Mor{\eta}{A}{\Lift{A}}#}. Proof. Let {#a\in A#} be an element of the dcpo {#A#}. To show that {#\eta_A a#} is positive, we fix a semidirected subset {#U\subseteq \Lift{A}#} such that {#\eta_A a\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup U#} to show that {#U#} is inhabited. The inequality implies that {#\top\sqsubseteq \exists u\in U. u{\downarrow}#}, so we know that {#U#} is inhabited.

item.title: the positive elements of a pointed dcpo
item.description: An element {#u#} of a pointed dpco {#A#} is called positive when for any semidirected subset {#I\subseteq A#}, if {#u\sqsubseteq \bigsqcup{I}#} then {#I#} is inhabited. We will write {#\IsPos,{u}#} to mean that {#u#} is a positive element. (This is adapted from Johnstone’s definition of positive elements of frames to the case of dcpos by Tom de Jong.)

item.title: algebra structure on the colimit apex
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a category and let {#\TMon = \prn{T,\eta,\mu}#} be a monad on {#\CCat#}, writing {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category {#\EM#}. Suppose that {#T#} preserves colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures for a given small category {#\ICat#}. Let {#\Mor{X_\bullet}{\ICat}{\EM}#} be a diagram in {#\EM#} such that {#\ICat\xrightarrow{X_\bullet}\EM\xrightarrow{U}\CCat#} has a universal cocone {#\Mor{c_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{C}}#}. We may lift {#C#} to an essentially unique {#\TMon#}-algebra structure {#\bar{C}#} with {#U\bar{C}=C#} in a canonical way such that {#\Mor{c_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{C}}#} lifts to a cocone of algebras {#\Mor{\bar{c}\Sub{\bullet}}{X\Sub{\bullet}}{\bar{C}}#} with {#U\bar{c}\bullet=c\bullet#}.

item.title: creation of colimits by the forgetful functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a category and let {#\TMon = \prn{T,\eta,\mu}#} be a monad on {#\CCat#}, writing {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category {#\EM#}. If {#T#} preserves colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures for a given small category {#\ICat#}, then {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} creates colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures. Proof. Let {#\Mor{X_\bullet}{\ICat}{\EM}#} be a diagram such that {#\ICat\xrightarrow{X_\bullet}\EM\xrightarrow{U}\CCat#} has a (co)limit cocone {#\Mor{c_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{C}}#}. Let {#\bar{C}=\prn{C,\alpha_{\bar{C}}}#} be the canonical algebra structure on the colimit apex {#C#} such that {#\Mor{c_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{C}}#} lifts to a cocone of algebras.

item.title: creation of (co)limits
item.description: Let {#\Mor{U}{\CCat}{\DCat}#} be a functor and let {#\ICat#} be a category. The functor {#U#} is said to create (co)limits of {#\ICat#}-figures when for any diagram {#\Mor{C_\bullet}{\ICat}{\CCat}#} such that {#\ICat\xrightarrow{C_\bullet}\CCat\xrightarrow{F}\DCat#} has a (co)limit, then {#C_\bullet#} has a (co)limit that is both preserved and reflected by {#F#}.

item.title: reflection of colimits by the forgetful functor
item.description: Let {#\CCat#} be a category and let {#\TMon = \prn{T,\eta,\mu}#} be a monad on {#\CCat#}, writing {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} for the forgetful functor from the Eilenberg–Moore category {#\EM#}. If {#T#} preserves colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures for a given indexing category {#\ICat#}, then {#\Mor{U}{\EM}{\CCat}#} reflects colimits of {#\ICat#}-figures. Proof. Let {#\Mor{X_\bullet}{\ICat}{\EM}#} be a diagram in {#\EM#} and let {#\Mor{y_\bullet}{X_\bullet}{\brc{Y}}#} be a cocone in {#\EM#} such that {#\Mor{Uy_\bullet}{UX_\bullet}{\brc{UY}}#} is a universal cocone for {#UX_\bullet = \ICat\xrightarrow{X_\bullet}\EM\xrightarrow{U}\CCat#}.

item.title: constructive domain theory
item.description: I am accumulating an understanding of constructive domain theory — that is, the domain theory that can be carried out in an elementary topos with a natural numbers object. These notes are based on the work of Tom de Jong and Martín Escardó; these notes, however, assume the propositional resizing principle whereas de Jong and Escardó explore a predicative version domain theory based on large and locally small domains. Results in Constructive Monad Theory [jms-002U] In this section, we gather some useful definitions and results concerning creation of (co)limits.

item.title: conventions of univalent mathematics
item.description: A category has hom sets, and is always assumed to be univalent / Rezk-complete; we use the term precategory to refer to a structure that has hom sets and a type of objects with no univalence condition.

item.title: polarized object
item.description: An object {#A#} in a deductive system is called polarized when it is either positive or negative.

item.title: preduploid
item.description: A deductive system {#\mathcal{D}#} is called a preduploid when every object {#A\in\mathcal{D}#} is polarized.

item.title: polarization and preduploids
item.description: Definition (polarized object) [dpl-000A] An object A in a deductive system is called polarized when it is either positive or negative. Definition (preduploid) [dpl-0009] A deductive system \mathcal{D} is called a preduploid when every object A\in\mathcal{D} is polarized.

item.title: characterization of full associativity
item.description: The following are equivalent for a deductive system {#\mathcal{D}#}: every map is linear; every map is thunkable; every object is positive; every object is negative; the cut law is associative; {#\mathcal{D}#} is a precategory. Proof. Supposing that every map is linear, we must check that each {#f:A\vdash B#} is thunkable. Fixing {#g:B\vdash C#} and {#h:C\vdash D#}, we must check that {#f;\prn{g;h} = \prn{f;g};h#}; as {#h#} is assumed linear, the equation holds.

item.title: deductive systems
item.description: The most basic construct of duploid theory is a deductive system, a variant on the concept of a precategory that relaxes the associativity axiom to reflect the evaluation order of execution in effectful programming languages and proof theories. In this section, we define deductive systems and develop their basic properties. Definition (deductive system) [dpl-0002] A deductive system \mathcal{D} is given by a type \Ob{\mathcal{D}} of “objects”, and for every pair A,B\in\Ob{\mathcal{D}} a set \Hom{\mathcal{D}}{A}{B} of morphisms, together with cut and identity laws:

item.title: positive and negative objects
item.description: An object {#B#} in a deductive system {#\mathcal{D}#} is called positive when every map {#B\vdash C#} is linear; it is called negative when every map {#A\vdash B#} is thunkable.

item.title: linear and thunkable maps
item.description: Let {#f:C \vdash D#} be a morphism in a deductive system {#\mathcal{D}#}. We say that {#f#} is linear when for all {#g:B\vdash C#} and {#h:A\vdash B#}, we have {#h;\prn{g;f} = \prn{h;g};f#}. We say that {#f#} is thunkable when for all {#g:D\vdash E#} and {#h : E\vdash F#}, we have {#f;\prn{g;h} = \prn{f;g};h#}.

item.title: deductive system notation
item.description: Given a deductive system {#\mathcal{D}#}, we will write {#A\in\mathcal{D}#} to mean {#A\in\Ob{\mathcal{D}}#} and {#f:A\vdash\Sub{\mathcal{D}} B#} when {#f\in\Hom{\mathcal{D}}{A}{B}#}. When {#\mathcal{D}#} is clear from context, we will write {#\vdash#} instead of {#\vdash\Sub{\mathcal{D}}#}.

item.title: deductive system
item.description: A deductive system {#\mathcal{D}#} is given by a type {#\Ob{\mathcal{D}}#} of “objects”, and for every pair {#A,B\in\Ob{\mathcal{D}}#} a set {#\Hom{\mathcal{D}}{A}{B}#} of morphisms, together with cut and identity laws: cut: for each {#A,B,C\in\Ob{\mathcal{D}}#} and {#f\in \Hom{\mathcal{D}}{A}{B}#} and {#g\in\Hom{\mathcal{D}}{B}{C}#}, a morphism {#f;g\in\Hom{\mathcal{D}}{A}{C}#}. identity: for each {#A\in\Ob{\mathcal{D}}#}, a morphism {#1\Sub{A}\in\Hom{\mathcal{D}}{A}{A}#} such that {#1\Sub{A};f = f#} and {#f;1\Sub{B}#} for each {#f\in\Hom{\mathcal{D}}{A}{B}#}.

item.title: notes on duploid theory
item.description: This is an investigation of Guillaume Munch-Maccagnoni’s theory of duploids, working in the setting of univalent foundations. Most of what we do could be done in non-univalent foundations; the purpose of univalence is to sharpen our perspective on the relationship between structure and property in duploid theory. Convention (conventions of univalent mathematics) [dpl-000B] A category has hom sets, and is always assumed to be univalent / Rezk-complete; we use the term precategory to refer to a structure that has hom sets and a type of objects with no univalence condition.
